claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'm here to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'm inquiring about my son Alex, who is 17 years old and lives with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask a few more questions.

Since Alex is 17 years old, **is he currently in approved education or training?** (For example, is he in school, college, or an approved training program?)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's still in college doing his A-levels.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's helpful, thank you. **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Just a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is Alex currently working 24 hours or more per week, or is he claiming any benefits in his own right such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work that much - he just focuses on his studies. And he's not claiming any benefits himself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. One final question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Based on the information you've provided:

- Alex is 17 years old and in approved education (college doing A-levels)
- You live with him in the UK, making you responsible for him
- He's not working 24+ hours per week
- He's not claiming benefits in his own right
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him

Since Alex is under 20 and staying in approved education, you continue to qualify for Child Benefit even though he's over 16.

**Please note:** If you or your partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may have to pay the High Income Child Benefit tax charge. You can still claim Child Benefit but may need to pay some or all of it back through your tax return.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit. If your circumstances change (such as Alex leaving education or starting work of 24+ hours per week), you'll need to inform HMRC.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is in approved education (college doing A-levels). Under the rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16, but eligibility continues if your child is under 20 and stays in approved education or training. Alex meets this criterion as he is 17 and in college. You live with Alex in the UK, making you responsible for him. He is not working 24 hours or more per week, not claiming benefits in his own right, and nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. Therefore, you meet all the eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 17-year-old son Alex, who is in approved education (college A-levels). Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not claiming benefits himself, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, not claiming benefits himself, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for him. The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
